metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the argument signature for `rebar trusted-env add|revoke` (e.g., `rebar trusted-env add <env-name> <key-file>` / `rebar trusted-env revoke <env-name> [--grandfather-at <log-pos>]`) so the helper AC test has a concrete invocation to assert against.
findings.finding: The `rebar trusted-env add|revoke` helper's CLI argument signature is never specified — an executor cannot implement the helper or write the AC test ('a test asserts … the helper stamps correctly') without knowing what arguments `add` and `revoke` accept (e.g., environment name, key-material path/fingerprint, optional log-position override).
findings.location: Additional Acceptance Criteria — 4th bullet: '`.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` era fields … operator helper (`rebar trusted-env add|revoke`) that stamps the current tickets tip; a test asserts … the helper stamps correctly'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line CLI synopsis per subcommand, e.g.: `rebar trusted-env add <env-name> <pubkey-path>` stamps `added_at_log_position` with the current tickets-branch tip; `rebar trusted-env revoke <env-name> [--grandfather-at <log-position>]` stamps `revoked_at_log_position` (and optionally `revoked_grandfather_pin`).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the YAML field that stores the revocation grandfather pin in `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` (e.g., `revoked_grandfather_pin: <log-position>`) so the rollback regression AC fixture has a concrete schema to populate.
findings.finding: The rollback regression AC tests a 'grandfather pin' scenario ('with a pin, certs stored at/before the pin stay valid') but the plan never names the YAML field that holds the pin value in `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml`, leaving the executor to invent the schema field.
findings.location: Additional Acceptance Criteria — 3rd bullet (rollback regression AC): 'with a pin, certs stored at/before the pin stay valid'; Remediation section: 'with a pin, certs stored at/before the pin stay valid (routine rotation preserves history)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either enumerate the expected `on:` triggers of `.github/workflows/verify-identity.yaml` directly in the plan (so the test has a fixed expected set) or specify that the test derives the authoritative trigger list by parsing the workflow's `on:` block and asserts 1-to-1 coverage with the comment's list.
findings.finding: The CI-trigger audit AC requires a test to assert the comment 'lists every trigger', but the plan never enumerates the expected triggers or states how many there are — making 'every trigger' an untestable cardinality claim (defect type 5: missing cardinality assertion).
findings.location: AC 4: 'a test asserts the audit comment is present and lists every trigger'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the AC: 'the test parses the workflow file's `on:` keys programmatically and asserts the audit comment names each one, so trigger additions automatically fail the test until the comment is updated.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_commands/trusted_env.py` (and any CLI dispatch wiring) to the Edit Targets section, or explicitly state where the `rebar trusted-env add|revoke` helper lives.
findings.finding: The `rebar trusted-env add|revoke` helper (described in Remediation Finding #5 and tested in the last Additional AC) is absent from the Edit Targets section, which only lists `verify_opcert.py`, `_cli/__init__.py`, `_config_schema.py`, and `.github/workflows/verify-identity.yaml`. An implementer following Edit Targets would miss this deliverable entirely.
findings.location: Remediation: Additional Acceptance Criteria (last bullet) vs. Edit Targets section
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Edit Targets: '`src/rebar/_commands/trusted_env.py` (new `rebar trusted-env add|revoke` subcommand that resolves and stamps the current tickets-branch tip as `added_at_log_position`/`revoked_at_log_position`); `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py` (dispatch arm for `trusted-env`).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the Per-ticket walk description to explicitly state that SIGNATURE events (carrying the op-cert envelope) must also be tracked per ticket so the gate can resolve `S` (the SIGNATURE event's introducing commit) for the key-validity era check.
findings.finding: The Per-ticket walk description in Context says the walk 'groups by `ticket_id`' over yielded events, but the Remediation (Finding #5) requires the gate to resolve `S` — the introducing commit of the envelope-bearing `SIGNATURE` event — from 'its own merged-log walk with the same fail-closed resolver.' Tracking `SIGNATURE` events per ticket to resolve `S` is a distinct walk concern not mentioned in the Per-ticket walk section, creating an underspecified gap between the two sections.
findings.location: Context: 'Per-ticket walk' section vs. Remediation Finding #5 'era anchor' section
findings.suggested_fix: In the Per-ticket walk section, add: 'For each ticket the walk also records the introducing commit of the `SIGNATURE` event bearing the `completion-verifier` op-cert (storage anchor `S`); this is passed to the key-validity era check alongside the close anchor.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/migration path for the `trusted_environments.yaml` era field format change: either document an expand-contract sequence (support both formats during a transition window) or provide a `rebar trusted-env migrate` command, and justify the breaking change as an explicit choice.
findings.finding: The era field format change in `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` — from 'legacy main-SHA' to `added_at_log_position`/`revoked_at_log_position` (tickets-branch log positions) — is a breaking schema change. The plan acknowledges the legacy format only via a test that surfaces a located error, but provides no rollback path, no expand-contract sequencing, and no migration guide for existing files that carry the old format.
findings.location: Remediation: Additional Acceptance Criteria (last bullet): 'a malformed/legacy main-SHA era field surfaces the existing located-error behavior'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR (or CLAUDE.md / design-doc section) capturing the era-at-storage-anchor (Option B) rationale, the breaking schema change from main-SHA to tickets-branch log positions, and the grandfather-pin semantics — and list it as an edit target alongside `verify_opcert.py`.
findings.finding: The era-at-storage-anchor design (Option B over Option A) and the breaking schema change to `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` (main-SHA → tickets-branch log positions) are novel architectural decisions with significant security and operability consequences, but the plan names no ADR, CLAUDE.md section, or design-doc update to capture the rationale for future maintainers.
findings.location: Remediation section (Finding #5 / era anchor) and Additional AC for `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` era fields
findings.suggested_fix: Add `.rebar/decisions/opcert-era-anchor.md` (or equivalent) as an explicit edit target in the plan, covering: (1) why Option B (storage anchor S) was chosen over Option A (cert-chosen anchor), (2) the `added_at_log_position`/`revoked_at_log_position` schema and why it must reference the tickets-branch DAG, (3) revocation-without-pin semantics. Reference it from the `trusted_environments.yaml` schema and from the `verify_opcert.py` module docstring.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the behavior for `trusted_environments.yaml` entries lacking `added_at_log_position`/`revoked_at_log_position` entirely (e.g., absent = valid-from-log-beginning with an explicit warning, or absent = error requiring migration), and add a corresponding AC assertion.
findings.finding: The plan introduces `added_at_log_position`/`revoked_at_log_position` era fields in `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` but does not specify the lifecycle for existing entries that have NO era fields at all. The AC only covers 'malformed/legacy main-SHA era field' (wrong type), not the absence of the field entirely. If absent fields error, every existing deployment breaks on upgrade; if they silently mean 'valid from the beginning of time,' that is a security regression (old keys become permanently valid with no era floor). The ownership/consumer-tolerance of this shared config file is therefore incomplete.
findings.location: Remediation section, Additional AC: '`.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` era fields are tickets-branch log positions … a malformed/legacy main-SHA era field surfaces the existing located-error behavior'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a migration rule: an entry with no `added_at_log_position` is treated as `added_at_log_position = 0` (valid from the beginning of the tickets log) and emits a deprecation warning; an entry with no `revoked_at_log_position` is treated as not revoked. Document this in the AC and add a test for the missing-field case.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add non-happy-path ACs for `rebar trusted-env add|revoke`: specify behavior and exit code when the environment name is not found, when `revoke` is called on an already-revoked key, and what the success/failure output looks like to the operator.
findings.finding: The `rebar trusted-env add|revoke` helper is a new operator-facing CLI that performs a security-critical action (stamping key validity era boundaries), but the plan specifies only the happy path ('stamps the current tickets tip'). Non-happy-path interactions are not covered: invalid/unknown environment name, `revoke` called on a key never added, concurrent invocations writing conflicting positions, and the output format on success/failure. Given this is a security operator tool, these are the highest-stakes interactions.
findings.location: Remediation section, Additional AC: 'operator helper (`rebar trusted-env add|revoke`) that stamps the current tickets tip; a test asserts the schema parses, the helper stamps correctly'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the plan title (and/or add a scope summary) to reflect that this ticket also delivers the `rebar trusted-env add|revoke` key-management helper and the `added_at_log_position`/`revoked_at_log_position` era-field schema — so reviewers and planners see the full scope from the headline.
findings.finding: The plan's title promises a 'merge-gate verification CI job over the merged log' but the body also delivers a new operator-facing command (`rebar trusted-env add|revoke`) with its own schema (`added_at_log_position`/`revoked_at_log_position` era fields) and actor (the operator who manages key lifecycle). This is work beyond what the title represents — the title under-specifies the full scope.
findings.location: Remediation section — 'Additional Acceptance Criteria (findings #4 + #5)', last AC bullet; also `rebar trusted-env` helper description in Finding #5 block
findings.suggested_fix: Retitle to something like: 'op-cert: merge-gate verification CI job + era-anchored key validity (storage-anchor + trusted-env operator helper)' — or add a one-line scope note at the top of the Context section listing both the gate and the key-management helper as deliverables.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge in the plan (as a note or explicit deferral decision) whether `rebar trusted-env add|revoke` is intentionally bundled in this ticket or could be split to a thin follow-on, given the gate's defined fail-closed behavior for unresolvable storage anchors.
findings.finding: The `rebar trusted-env add|revoke` helper has a distinct actor (operator managing key trust lifecycle) and is independently testable/releasable relative to the verification gate — the gate can ship failing-closed (unresolvable storage anchor → current validity required, per spec) while the helper ships in a follow-on. This means the ticket bundles two actors' concerns in one unit, a weak decomposition signal worth surfacing.
findings.location: Remediation section — Finding #5 description; last remediation AC bullet

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen the CI-trigger audit AC to require the test to independently enumerate the workflow's `on:` trigger keys (by parsing the YAML) and assert each key is mentioned in the comment, rather than only checking the comment block exists — making the test fail when triggers are added without updating the audit comment.
findings.finding: The CI-trigger audit test is specified as asserting the comment 'is present and lists every trigger,' but the AC does not require the test to independently parse the workflow's `on:` block and cross-check the comment's completeness. As written, an implementer can satisfy this AC by grepping for the comment block's existence — locking in whatever the author wrote, even if the comment misclassifies or omits a trigger. This is a source-grep masquerading as a behavioral test (change-detector / tautology anti-pattern).
findings.location: AC: 'CI-trigger audit as a concrete artifact: … a test asserts the audit comment is present and lists every trigger.'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'a test asserts the audit comment is present and lists every trigger' with: 'a test parses the `on:` section of the workflow YAML, extracts all trigger event names, and asserts each appears in the audit comment block — so the test fails if a trigger is added without updating the comment.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `_collect_all`, `_collect_range`, and `_ScopedEvent` are extracted into a shared internal module (e.g., `rebar._commands._verify_log`) before `verify_opcert.py` imports them — so both gates share the abstraction with a stable, non-private API rather than crossing a private module boundary.
findings.finding: The plan says `verify_opcert.py` will 'reuse' `_collect_all`, `_collect_range`, and `_ScopedEvent` from `verify_authorship.py`, but these are module-private symbols (underscore-prefixed) with exactly one call-site each (within `verify_authorship.py`'s own `cli()`). The plan does not specify whether to extract them into a shared internal module — leaving implementors to either cross-import private internals (tight coupling to an implementation detail) or copy-paste them (NIH). Neither satisfies Rule-of-Three (≥3 call-sites needed to justify a shared abstraction).
findings.location: Context § 'Per-ticket walk'; Edit Targets § 'src/rebar/_commands/verify_opcert.py'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an Edit Target: `src/rebar/_commands/_verify_log.py` (new shared module, extracted from `verify_authorship.py`), exporting `ScopedEvent`, `collect_all`, `collect_range`, `is_enforced`, and `resolve_commit`. Both `verify_authorship.py` and `verify_opcert.py` import from it. This gives the shared helpers ≥2 call-sites at ship time, with a clear path to the Rule-of-Three threshold.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether the storage-anchor era validity check for trusted-environment keys can reuse `authorship.resolve_event_commit` and the `git merge-base --is-ancestor` primitives from `authorship.py`, or explain in the plan why a parallel implementation is necessary (e.g., the SIGNATURE event's path structure differs from a `{timestamp}-{uuid}` event file).
findings.finding: The era-at-storage-anchor design (Finding #5 remediation) rebuilds commit-ancestry era machinery — position → commit SHA via `resolve_event_commit`, then `_is_ancestor` via `git merge-base --is-ancestor` — that already exists in `src/rebar/attest/authorship.py` for the identity keyring (`verify_authorship_at_commit`, lines 241–333). The new `added_at_log_position`/`revoked_at_log_position` fields and the `rebar trusted-env` helper will necessarily re-implement this position-resolution + ancestry-check logic for a different schema object (`.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml`). The plan does not explain why the existing `resolve_event_commit` + `_is_ancestor` primitives cannot be reused for the storage-anchor validity check.
findings.location: Remediation § Finding #5; Additional AC § 'Rollback regression' and '`.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` era fields'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `opcert.opcert_from_record` (and its parent module) actually exists in the codebase or will be delivered as a hard prerequisite before this story starts; document the full import path and function signature in the plan.
findings.finding: `opcert.opcert_from_record` is asserted to exist and read back `(envelope, {material_fingerprint, merged_log_commit})` from the stored attestation record (attributed to 'keystone e4df'), but no `opcert` module and no `opcert_from_record` function exist anywhere in the codebase.
findings.location: Context section: 'An op-cert is stored on a ticket as an `attestations[<kind>]` record … `opcert.opcert_from_record` reads it back'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit 'Prerequisites / Dependencies' section listing `keystone e4df` with the concrete module path (e.g. `rebar.attest.opcert`) and confirm it lands before this story's implementation begins.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `sign_opcert_manifest` is a new function to be created by this story or a prerequisite from e4df; if the latter, probe that it exists before merging the plan, and update the fixture description to use the correct function name.
findings.finding: `signing.sign_opcert_manifest` is asserted to write op-cert envelopes (attributed to 'keystone e4df'), but no function named `sign_opcert_manifest` exists anywhere in the codebase — the existing signing entry-point for all attestation kinds is `signing.sign_manifest`.
findings.location: Context section: '`signing.sign_opcert_manifest` writes it'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either confirm `verify.require_environment` was genuinely delivered by ticket 42d1 and probe the installed schema to find it, or add it to `VerifyConfig` + `_SECTIONS['verify']` as an explicit edit target and AC in this story.
findings.finding: `verify.require_environment` is described as 'already shipped in 42d1' (a scope-exclusion claim), but the field is absent from `VerifyConfig` and absent from the `_SECTIONS['verify']` coercion table in `src/rebar/_config_schema.py`.
findings.location: Context section: '`verify.require_environment` (which environment must sign; already shipped in 42d1) is the posture'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `require_environment: str | None = None` to `VerifyConfig` and a corresponding `lambda v, k: _as_str(v, k)` entry in `_SECTIONS['verify']`, and list `_config_schema.py` edit for this field explicitly in Edit Targets.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Identify the actual field name(s) in the signed op-cert payload that carry the commit anchor (or specify that they will be introduced by e4df), and update all AC references to use the verified field name.
findings.finding: The `merged_log_commit` field is asserted to be part of the signed op-cert payload (read from the verified envelope by the gate), but no field by that name exists anywhere in the codebase — neither in signing records, reducer processors, nor any attestation schema.
findings.location: Context section (op-cert storage shape): '`opcert_from_record` reads it back to `(envelope, {material_fingerprint, merged_log_commit})`'; Additional AC (Finding #4 commit half): 'a bound commit that is not an ancestor of the gated history FAILS closed'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` and the `added_at_log_position`/`revoked_at_log_position` schema are delivered by a prerequisite story or must be created here; add the schema definition and the `rebar trusted-env` CLI arm as explicit edit targets with their own ACs.
findings.finding: `added_at_log_position` / `revoked_at_log_position` era fields are asserted to exist (or be introduced) in `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml`, but that file does not exist in the repository and neither field name appears anywhere in the codebase.
findings.location: Remediation section (Finding #5): 'Era boundaries in `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` become tickets-branch log positions (`added_at_log_position` / `revoked_at_log_position`)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `scripts/gen_cli_reference.py` (`INTERCEPT_COMMANDS` dict entry for `verify-opcert`) and `tests/unit/test_gen_cli_reference.py` (`_LADDER` set) to the enumerated edit targets, so the CLI-reference parity self-check and its drift-gate test stay green.
findings.finding: Adding a `verify-opcert` intercept arm to `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py` requires co-editing `scripts/gen_cli_reference.py` (its `INTERCEPT_COMMANDS` dict) and `tests/unit/test_gen_cli_reference.py` (its `_LADDER` set), neither of which the plan enumerates. The `render()` parity self-check raises `ValueError` when the intercept ladder and `INTERCEPT_COMMANDS` diverge, causing CI to fail.
findings.location: Edit targets section / src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py arm
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Edit targets section: 'scripts/gen_cli_reference.py (add a verify-opcert entry to INTERCEPT_COMMANDS); tests/unit/test_gen_cli_reference.py (add "verify-opcert" to _LADDER)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the edit targets for the `rebar trusted-env` helper: the new intercept/dispatch arm in `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py`, entry in `scripts/gen_cli_reference.py` (`INTERCEPT_COMMANDS`), and entry in `tests/unit/test_gen_cli_reference.py` (`_LADDER`).
findings.finding: The Additional Acceptance Criteria name a new `rebar trusted-env add|revoke` CLI helper but do not enumerate its required edit targets: an intercept arm (or dispatch arm) in `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py`, and corresponding entries in `scripts/gen_cli_reference.py` and `tests/unit/test_gen_cli_reference.py`. The same parity-self-check mechanism applies.
findings.location: Additional Acceptance Criteria — `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` era fields bullet

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/attest/trusted_env.py` (exporting `verify_required_environment`) and `src/rebar/attest/opcert.py` (exporting `opcert_from_record`) to the Edit Targets list, or cite the story that already creates them and pin their exact call signatures so the implementer can complete the AC without guessing.
findings.finding: The AC's call targets `rebar.attest.trusted_env.verify_required_environment` and `opcert.opcert_from_record` do not exist anywhere in the codebase and are absent from the plan's Edit Targets list — an implementer has no path to resolve these dependencies without guessing module boundaries and signatures.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria §1 ('verifies its required-kind op-cert via `rebar.attest.trusted_env.verify_required_environment` (reading the stored op-cert with `opcert.opcert_from_record`)'); Edit Targets section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly specify which git worktree (main-branch repo root vs. `.tickets-tracker` tickets-branch worktree) each ancestry/era check runs against: `merged_log_commit` ancestor-of-main-HEAD check, the storage-anchor `S` position comparison, and the `opcert_enforce_since` grandfathering check.
findings.finding: The plan conflates two distinct git histories — the code branch (`main`) for the `merged_log_commit` ancestor check and the tickets branch for the storage anchor `S` and `opcert_enforce_since` — without specifying which git worktree each ancestry check runs against, creating an ambiguous and potentially incorrect mechanism.
findings.location: Remediation §Finding #4 (commit half): 'constrained to resolve to a real ancestor of the gated `main` HEAD'; §Finding #5: 'storage anchor `S` — the introducing commit of the envelope-bearing `SIGNATURE` event, resolved by the gate from its own merged-log walk'; Additional AC §merged_log_commit bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Clarify: `merged_log_commit` ancestor check uses the main repo root (`git -C <repo_root> merge-base --is-ancestor <commit> HEAD`); storage anchor `S` and `opcert_enforce_since` both use the tickets-branch worktree (`git -C .tickets-tracker merge-base --is-ancestor`); era boundary log-position comparisons are plain string comparisons against tickets-branch commit SHAs (no cross-branch ancestry needed).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale explaining why `rebar.attest.trusted_env.verify_required_environment` is the right abstraction for op-cert environment-key verification rather than registering a new policy kind in the existing `registry.POLICY` table and calling `registry.verify` directly from `verify_opcert.py`.
findings.finding: The plan provides no positive rationale for introducing `rebar.attest.trusted_env.verify_required_environment` as a new module rather than extending the existing `signing.verify_signature` / `registry.verify` infrastructure — leaving the implementer without guidance on the design boundary and risking duplication or inconsistency with the existing attestation verification path.
findings.location: Context section ('verify its required-kind op-cert via `rebar.attest.trusted_env.verify_required_environment`'); Edit Targets section (no mention of `trusted_env`)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR (or a named section in ADR 0046 / CLAUDE.md) capturing the era-at-storage-anchor (Option B) rationale — specifically why key validity is evaluated at the SIGNATURE event's storage anchor S rather than at merged_log_commit, the threat it closes (revoked-key holder freshly signs, choosing a pre-revocation ancestor), and why this differs from gittuf's monotonic-counter fix — and list that ADR/doc as an edit target.
findings.finding: The era-at-storage-anchor (Option B) design — evaluating key validity at the tickets-branch commit S that introduced the SIGNATURE event rather than at merged_log_commit — is the plan's central novel architectural choice crossing the security/auth bright-line, but no ADR, CLAUDE.md section, or design-doc update is planned to record its rationale. ADR 0046 L1 documents the *existing* verify_authorship_at_commit model; it does not cover Option B. The plan mentions 'Option A' only by contrast (implicitly: validating at merged_log_commit) without naming it. Without a durable rationale record, a future contributor seeing the storage-anchor evaluation could change it back to merged_log_commit (the 'simpler' path), reintroducing the rollback gap.
findings.location: Remediation section — Finding #5 (era anchor) / Option B description

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the capability assertions for `rebar.attest.trusted_env.verify_required_environment`, `opcert.opcert_from_record`, and `sign_opcert_manifest` against the actual delivered interfaces from stories 42d1/e4df/ee0b — either confirm the exact module paths and call signatures match the plan's assertions, or list this plan as explicitly blocked on those stories and adjust the AC to match the confirmed interface.
findings.finding: AC1 and the fixture AC assert specific capability contracts for three symbols that do not exist in the current codebase (`rebar.attest.trusted_env.verify_required_environment`, `opcert.opcert_from_record`, and `sign_opcert_manifest`), referencing upstream stories (42d1, e4df, ee0b/8f1d) as producers. The plan asserts a concrete return contract for `opcert_from_record` — '(envelope, {material_fingerprint, merged_log_commit})' — and a specific invocation pattern for `verify_required_environment`. If those upstreams land with different signatures or module paths, this plan's implementation and tests will fail to compile/import with no plan-level flag. The plan does not specify a dependency-readiness gate (e.g., 'blocked on e4df merging') or provide a stub/interface contract against which this plan can be independently verified.
findings.location: AC1 ('verifies its required-kind op-cert via `rebar.attest.trusted_env.verify_required_environment` (reading the stored op-cert with `opcert.opcert_from_record`)') and AC3 fixture ('via `sign_opcert_manifest`')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an expand-contract migration path for `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` era fields: specify a transitional period where both legacy `main`-SHA era fields and new tickets-branch log-position fields are accepted (with a deprecation warning for the old format), document the operator conversion procedure (e.g., `rebar trusted-env migrate`), and only error on legacy fields after an announced cutover.
findings.finding: The `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` era-field schema change (main-SHA positions → tickets-branch log positions) is specified as a hard cut-over: the plan states old/legacy main-SHA era fields 'surface the existing located-error behavior,' meaning existing files with the old format immediately break. No expand-contract path (add new fields alongside old, backfill, then enforce) is described, violating the online/expand-contract requirement.
findings.location: Remediation section — 'era boundaries in `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` become tickets-branch log positions … a malformed/legacy main-SHA era field surfaces the existing located-error behavior'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assert the post-rollback schema state for `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml`: either (a) specify that old rebar binaries tolerate unknown era fields (unknown-key-ignore policy), documenting this as the back-out path, or (b) provide a `rebar trusted-env downgrade` / revert procedure and assert that reverting the YAML to old format is lossless; explicitly note `DATA LOSS RISK` if era-boundary history cannot be recovered after downgrade.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no post-rollback schema-state assertion for `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml`. If an operator converts their YAML to the new tickets-branch log-position format and then rolls back the rebar binary, the plan does not state whether the old binary can read the new format or what the schema state is after rollback — leaving a potential scenario where the file is unreadable by the rolled-back binary with no documented recovery path.
findings.location: Remediation section — Additional AC for `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` era fields; no rollback/back-out section present

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify and test idempotency of `rebar trusted-env add|revoke`: document and assert (in the helper's AC test) that re-running the command for an already-added/revoked key either no-ops (preserving the original log position) or errors, and that a partial failure leaves the YAML in a state the helper can safely re-run against.
findings.finding: The `rebar trusted-env add|revoke` helper's idempotency is unspecified: the plan says it 'stamps the current tickets tip' but does not state what happens if the helper is run twice for the same key (e.g., two operators race, or the command is re-run after a partial failure). A non-idempotent helper could produce duplicate or inconsistent era-boundary entries.
findings.location: Remediation section — 'operator-stamped at add/revoke time via a `rebar trusted-env` helper'; Additional AC — 'the helper stamps correctly'
findings.suggested_fix: The safest behavior: if a key already has `added_at_log_position` / `revoked_at_log_position` set, the helper errors with 'already stamped' rather than overwriting, requiring explicit `--force` to re-stamp. Document this in the AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_commands/trusted_env_cmd.py` (or equivalent) and a `verify-opcert` / `trusted-env` intercept arm in `_cli/__init__.py` to the Edit Targets section, so the `rebar trusted-env add|revoke` helper required by the last Additional AC has a named implementation path.
findings.finding: The `rebar trusted-env add|revoke` helper subcommand required by the last Additional AC (and the era-at-storage-anchor Remediation design) has no implementation trace in the repo (`_cli/__init__.py` has no `trusted-env` arm, `_commands/` has no `trusted_env.py`) and is absent from the plan's Edit Targets section — the plan names four edit targets but omits this command entirely.
findings.location: Edit Targets section; Additional AC: '`rebar trusted-env add|revoke` … a test asserts the schema parses, the helper stamps correctly'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Edit Targets: `src/rebar/_commands/trusted_env_cmd.py` (new CLI owning `rebar trusted-env add|revoke`, stamps `added_at_log_position`/`revoked_at_log_position` at current tickets tip) and a `trusted-env` equality-test intercept arm in `src/rebar/_cli/__init__.py`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/attest/trusted_env.py` to the Edit Targets section and specify whether the schema change is a hard cut-over (error on old fields) or supports a read shim, and add a migration note for any existing entries using the 42d1 `added_at_commit`/`revoked_at_commit` fields.
findings.finding: The Remediation section introduces a breaking schema change to `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` — replacing `added_at_commit`/`revoked_at_commit` (main-branch SHAs, as shipped by story 42d1) with `added_at_log_position`/`revoked_at_log_position` (tickets-branch log positions) — but names no migration path for existing entries and no edit target for updating the loader in `trusted_env.py`. The plan's final AC says 'a malformed/legacy main-SHA era field surfaces the existing located-error behavior,' implying old fields become hard errors, yet no migration guide or backward-compat read shim is specified.
findings.location: Remediation Finding #5; Additional AC: '.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml era fields are tickets-branch log positions … a malformed/legacy main-SHA era field surfaces the existing located-error behavior'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the access/trust contract for config-file inputs (`rebar.toml`, `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml`) in the CI workflow: specify that on any PR-class trigger the verifier reads these files exclusively from the gated base/merge-target branch ref (not the PR working tree), and add this constraint to the CI-trigger audit comment and to the `trusted_environments.yaml` schema section.
findings.finding: The plan does not declare the trust posture of the verifier's configuration inputs — specifically `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` (the era-anchor and key-validity source) and `rebar.toml` — with respect to the CI checkout context. If the workflow is triggered by a `pull_request`-class event from a fork (a lower-trust actor), those files are read from the attacker-controlled working tree rather than the gated/merged base-branch state, allowing a fork contributor to substitute a forged `trusted_environments.yaml` that declares their own key as trusted and zeroes out revocation positions, bypassing the entire Finding #5 era-validity control.
findings.location: AC line 4 (CI-trigger audit) and Remediation / Finding #5 section; `.github/workflows/verify-identity.yaml` step
findings.suggested_fix: In the `.github/workflows/verify-identity.yaml` op-cert step, add an explicit checkout or path-pinning step that ensures `trusted_environments.yaml` and `rebar.toml` are sourced from `${{ github.base_ref }}` / the merge-target SHA rather than the PR branch, OR restrict the workflow so the op-cert step only fires on `push` to the protected branch (post-merge), with a clear AC statement that pre-merge PR triggers are explicitly out of scope for the op-cert lane.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `rebar.attest.trusted_env` (or the actual module path) to Edit Targets and specify its `verify_required_environment` function signature, return type, and error contract (raised exception vs. verdict enum) so AC1 is implementable without guessing.
findings.finding: `rebar.attest.trusted_env` (the module housing `verify_required_environment`) is referenced as an existing call target in AC1 but does not exist anywhere in the codebase and is absent from Edit Targets — an executor cannot implement `verify_opcert.py` without knowing this module's signature, error contract, and where it is created.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1: '…verifies its required-kind op-cert via `rebar.attest.trusted_env.verify_required_environment`…'; Edit Targets section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the `opcert` module (or its actual location) to Edit Targets, define `opcert_from_record`'s signature and the exact shape of its return tuple, and confirm `sign_opcert_manifest` from `signing` is already present or add it to Edit Targets.
findings.finding: `opcert.opcert_from_record` is referenced as the reader function for the stored op-cert (returning `(envelope, {material_fingerprint, merged_log_commit})`), but the module `opcert` does not exist in the codebase and is not listed in Edit Targets.
findings.location: Context section: '`opcert.opcert_from_record` reads it back to `(envelope, {material_fingerprint, merged_log_commit})`'; AC1: 'reading the stored op-cert with `opcert.opcert_from_record`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the type, default, coercer, and env-override name for BOTH `verify.opcert_enforce_since` (string/git-ref, mirroring `identity.enforce_since`) and `verify.require_environment` (string environment name) in the `_config_schema.py` edit target, and confirm or refute the '42d1 already shipped' claim.
findings.finding: The two new config keys `verify.opcert_enforce_since` and `verify.require_environment` are specified as the posture/boundary inputs for `rebar verify-opcert`, but neither key exists in `VerifyConfig` or `_SECTIONS['verify']` in `_config_schema.py`, and the plan's Edit Targets entry for `_config_schema.py` says only 'add `verify.opcert_enforce_since`' — omitting `verify.require_environment` and providing no coercer, default value, or env-override specification for either.
findings.location: Context: 'Add `verify.opcert_enforce_since`…; `verify.require_environment`…is the posture (already shipped in 42d1)'; Edit Targets: '`src/rebar/_config_schema.py` (add `verify.opcert_enforce_since`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add both to `VerifyConfig` and `_SECTIONS['verify']` analogously to `identity.enforce_since` (`_as_str`) and document the env-override names (e.g. `REBAR_VERIFY_REQUIRE_ENVIRONMENT`, `REBAR_VERIFY_OPCERT_ENFORCE_SINCE`).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the concrete type of `added_at_log_position`/`revoked_at_log_position` (commit SHA, position string, or integer) and the exact comparison operation used to decide 'key valid at anchor S', co-located with the `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` schema description.
findings.finding: The `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` era-field schema (`added_at_log_position` / `revoked_at_log_position`) is described only as 'tickets-branch log positions' with 'plain ancestry comparison', but the plan never specifies the concrete field format (a commit SHA? a `{timestamp}-{uuid}` position string? a POSIX integer?), making the decision rule for 'is key valid at storage anchor S' unimplementable and un-testable by an executor without guessing the type.
findings.location: Remediation Finding #5: 'Era boundaries in `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` become tickets-branch log positions (`added_at_log_position` / `revoked_at_log_position`), operator-stamped at add/revoke time via a `rebar trusted-env` helper — the same DAG as `S`, plain ancestry comparison.'
findings.suggested_fix: Specify that the fields are tickets-branch commit SHAs (stamped by `rebar trusted-env add|revoke` as the current `git rev-parse tickets`) and that validity is decided by `git merge-base --is-ancestor added_at_log_position S`, mirroring the `verify_authorship_at_commit` pattern already in `authorship.py`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact decision rule for 'currently valid' under the new era model when the storage anchor S is unresolvable — e.g. 'key is currently valid iff `revoked_at_log_position` is absent in trusted_environments.yaml' — and co-locate this fallback rule with the schema definition.
findings.finding: The fallback for an unresolvable storage anchor `S` is specified as 'key must then be currently valid', but the plan does not define what 'currently valid' means under the new `added_at_log_position`/`revoked_at_log_position` era model — the prior model used identity-ticket keyring events, but the new model uses a YAML file with log-position fields. The fallback decision rule is not co-located with either schema.
findings.location: Remediation Finding #5: 'an unresolvable/compacted anchor fails closed (the key must then be currently valid)'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
